summary: Bryan Cantrill memorializes John Birrell, a FreeBSD developer who passed away after a stroke on November 20, 2009. Birrell was the first to port DTrace to FreeBSD, a significant technical achievement that required mastering both DTrace internals and the target operating system. Cantrill emphasizes Birrell's exceptional tenacity—his willingness to tackle unnecessary but correct technical challenges, and his persistence through frustrating licensing obstacles that required significant rework.
key_points: John Birrell was the first to port DTrace to FreeBSD, a significant technical undertaking
Birrell went beyond requirements by also porting the cyclic subsystem for completeness
When licensing issues arose, Birrell found creative solutions requiring additional technical work
His legacy will benefit future generations even if they never know his name
money_quotes: I have long believed that the mark of a great engineer is not how frequently they get knocked down, but rather how quickly they get back up – and in this regard, John was indisputably a giant.
DTrace passes through rough country, and a port to a foreign system is a significant undertaking that requires mastery of both DTrace and (particularly) the target system.
That he undertook this additional technical challenge anyway – even when any victory would remain hidden to all but the most expert eye – says a lot about John as both an engineer and a man.
While your legacy might remain anonymous to the future generations that will benefit from the fruits of your long labor, we will always know that it never would have happened without you.
key_insight: True engineering excellence is measured not by avoiding obstacles but by the resilience and tenacity to overcome them, often doing the harder right thing even when no one will notice.
